quote:
"""Singleton server management for multi-session environments.

When multiple Claude Code sessions start simultaneously, this module ensures
only ONE HTTP server process is spawned. All sessions connect to it as
lightweight stdio proxies.

Race condition handling:
  - File lock ensures exactly one process starts the server
  - Losers of the lock race poll until the server is healthy
  - Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd on health checks
  - Fallback to standalone stdio mode if server fails to start
"""
what: The module is named and documented as covering only the "singleton" dedup guarantee (one backend process across concurrent sessions), but the 571-line file actually implements five distinct subsystems under that one name: file-lock singleton election (_exclusive_lock, _start_backend_holding_lock), version-aware backend discovery/eviction (_find_running_server, _clear_stale_backend, _is_our_backend), an HTTP readiness prober (_await_backend_http), a full bidirectional stdio-to-streamable-HTTP proxy bridge (_proxy_streams, ~150 lines handling session-id capture, initialize-swallowing, task groups), and a standalone liveness watchdog (_watch_backend_liveness). None of that breadth is signaled by the name "singleton.py" or its docstring.
why_it_matters: Every Stage-2 fix plan reviewed so far routes through this one file by line number (M1 liveness at singleton.py:83, M2 reuse-key at singleton.py:132,263, M3 observability touches the same proxy code, M7/M8 recovery work is adjacent). An agent asked to "add a correlation id to backend requests" or "fix the stdio proxy's reconnect logic" has no naming signal that this is the file to open — "singleton" reads as pure dedup bookkeeping, not the transport layer. The mismatch actively misroutes exactly the kind of edits this audit's own roadmap prioritizes next.
fix_direction: Split by responsibility into modules whose names match their content, e.g. backend_lifecycle.py (election/discovery/eviction/version-gate) and stdio_proxy.py (the streams bridge + watchdog). If a split is too disruptive right now, at minimum retitle the module docstring to enumerate its real scope, or rename the file itself (e.g. backend_singleton_and_proxy.py) so navigation-by-name works without opening it.

quote:
"""Singleton server management for multi-session environments.

When multiple Claude Code sessions start simultaneously, this module ensures
only ONE HTTP server process is spawned. All sessions connect to it as
lightweight stdio proxies.
    print(f"session root: {root}  (exists: {root.exists()})")
    print(f"clone cap   : {_human(server._clone_storage_cap_bytes())}  [STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_STORAGE_CAP_GB]")
    print(f"session cap : {_human(server._session_storage_cap_bytes())}  [STEALTH_MCP_SESSION_STORAGE_CAP_GB]")
what: "session" denotes at least three unrelated things depending on which module you're reading, and two collide directly in the CLI's own `status` output. (1) An MCP client/stdio connection — singleton.py's docstring frames the whole file around "multi-session environments" where a "session" is one Claude Code stdio connection ("All sessions connect to it as lightweight stdio proxies"). (2) A named browser-profile-clone directory under the session root — cli.py's `status` command prints "session root" and "session cap" (backed by `_default_session_root()` / `_session_storage_cap_bytes()`), referring purely to disk-cap bookkeeping for cloned Chrome profiles (see also server.py:1051-1052's `sessions/github-session` naming), unrelated to any MCP connection. (3) The current backend process's own run/lifetime — process_cleanup.py:361 speaks of processes that "pre-date this server session", a third, purely process-lifecycle sense. Separately, "proxy" is similarly overloaded: proxy_forwarder.py ("Authenticated proxy forwarding for browser launch proxies") and proxy_utils.py ("Proxy parsing and Chrome launch-arg helpers") use it for the outbound HTTP/SOCKS proxy a spawned browser routes traffic through, while singleton.py uses the identical word for the inbound stdio-to-HTTP bridge process itself ("lightweight stdio proxies", `run_stdio_proxy`, `_proxy_streams`). Neither pair of "proxy" modules shares any code path with the other.
why_it_matters: A person reading `stealth-chrome-devtools status` sees "session root"/"session cap" printed right below backend/version lines with no cue that this "session" has nothing to do with their current MCP session — they could tune STEALTH_MCP_SESSION_STORAGE_CAP_GB expecting it to affect connection/backend behavior when it only trims cloned-profile directories. Symmetrically, an agent asked to "add per-proxy auth retry logic" or "fix the proxy reconnect" has no naming signal to tell which of two unrelated subsystems (browser egress proxy vs. MCP stdio proxy) is meant before it can even choose a file.
fix_direction: Reserve "session" for one concept codebase-wide (recommend keeping it for the profile-clone directories, since that's the persisted/CLI-facing term) and rename the other senses — e.g. `connection` for the MCP stdio-to-backend link, `run`/`process_epoch` for process-lifetime scoping in process_cleanup.py. Rename proxy_forwarder.py/proxy_utils.py's concept to `egress_proxy`/`upstream_proxy` (matching the existing `ProxyConfig` naming) to free "proxy" for the stdio bridge alone, or vice versa. A short glossary section in CLAUDE.md/DESIGN.md pinning one meaning per term would prevent recurrence.

quote:
"""Response handler for managing large responses and automatic file-based fallbacks."""
"""
Response Stage Hook Processing - Extension for Dynamic Hook System

This module adds response-stage interception and modification capabilities
to the existing dynamic hook system. It allows AI-generated functions to
modify response content, headers, and status codes.
"""
what: Two adjacently-alphabetized files in the same directory both center on "response" but mean entirely different things by it. response_handler.py's "response" is the MCP tool-call return payload — it exists purely to detect an oversized dict/list about to be returned to the LLM and spill it to a file instead (`handle_response`, `estimate_tokens`, `default_clone_output_dir`). response_stage_hooks.py's "response" is the intercepted HTTP network response for the page's own network traffic — it lets an AI-authored dynamic hook rewrite a website's response body/headers/status before nodriver delivers it to the page (`ResponseStageProcessor.execute_response_action`, imports `HookAction`/`RequestInfo` from dynamic_hook_system). Neither imports nor calls the other; they are unrelated subsystems that happen to share a directory and a leading word.
why_it_matters: An agent told "there's a bug in the response handling" or grepping `response_` to find where to add a field has two same-prefixed files to choose between with no way to tell from the name which "response" is meant. response_handler.py is the wrong file for anything network/hook-related despite being the more literal match for "response handler". Given a directory listing or grep surfaces both with no ranking signal, a misrouted edit is a real risk on this single-user tool where such a mistake would ship silently.
fix_direction: Rename one to remove the ambiguity — e.g. response_stage_hooks.py -> network_response_hooks.py (matching the existing network_interceptor.py naming family) or http_response_hooks.py — leaving response_handler.py's "response" unambiguously the MCP tool-response formatter.

quote:
async def execute_script(
    """
    Execute JavaScript in the page and return its value.
async def inject_and_execute_script(
    """
    Inject and execute custom JavaScript code.
async def execute_python_in_browser(
    """
    Execute Python code by translating it to JavaScript.
async def call_javascript_function(
    """
    Call a JavaScript function with arguments.
what: Four of the 96 AI-facing tools all mean "run some code in the page" and their docstrings give almost no differentiating signal: `execute_script` ("Execute JavaScript in the page and return its value") vs `inject_and_execute_script` ("Inject and execute custom JavaScript code") read as paraphrases of the same sentence — both take a JS string + args and return a dict. `execute_python_in_browser` sounds like it runs Python server-side against the browser, but its own docstring says it "translat[es] it to JavaScript" — it is also a JS-execution path, just with a Python-syntax frontend. `call_javascript_function` is narrower (calls an already-discovered function by path) but the verb — call vs execute vs inject-and-execute — carries no consistent meaning across the cluster. `execute_cdp_command` (line 3561, "Execute any CDP Runtime command") and `execute_function_sequence` (line 3798, "Execute a sequence of JavaScript function calls") extend the same cluster with two more "execute" verbs for yet other scopes.
why_it_matters: This is the kind of tool-surface ambiguity an agent has to burn a turn resolving: with 6 similarly-named "run code" tools and no behavioral cue in the names, choosing the right one for "run this JS snippet" requires opening and diffing 3-4 docstrings rather than picking correctly from the tool list alone — exactly the bar this lens is checking against.
fix_direction: Rename to encode the actual distinguishing axis: keep `execute_script` as the canonical path; fold `inject_and_execute_script` into it or rename to `execute_script_in_context` (its one real differentiator is the optional context_id); rename `execute_python_in_browser` to `execute_python_as_js` or add "(experimental JS transpile)" to its docstring's first line; rename `call_javascript_function` to `call_discovered_function` (matching its own internal call to `cdp_function_executor.call_discovered_function`, already a better name one layer down).

quote:
async def clone_element_complete(
    """
    Master function that extracts ALL element data using specialized functions.
async def clone_element_to_file(
    """
    Clone element completely and save to file, returning file path instead of full data.
async def extract_complete_element_to_file(
    """
    Extract complete element using working comprehensive cloner and save to file.
async def extract_complete_element_cdp(
    """
    Extract complete element using native CDP methods for 100% accuracy.
what: Four tools claim to do the same thing — get "complete"/"ALL" element data — using "clone" and "extract" interchangeably with no naming signal for which is authoritative. `clone_element_complete`'s own docstring says it "extracts ALL element data" (uses "extract" to describe what "clone_" does). `clone_element_to_file` is the to-file twin of `file_based_element_cloner.clone_element_complete_to_file`, a *different* underlying function than the one behind `extract_complete_element_to_file` (`file_based_element_cloner.extract_complete_element_to_file`) — the same module mirrors the identical clone/extract split one layer down with no documented reason. Only `extract_complete_element_cdp`'s docstring body (not its name) reveals it is actually the most accurate variant ("bypassing JavaScript limitations… most accurate element cloning possible") — that ranking is invisible from any of the four names alone.
why_it_matters: This is a naming-navigability problem distinct from the audit's existing M5 (duplicate cloner implementations, ~35-40% removable LOC): even after M5's consolidation work, whichever tools survive still need names that tell an agent which one is "the accurate one" or "the file-safe one" without reading four docstring bodies word-for-word. Right now the verb choice alone (clone vs extract) gives zero signal, and it's inconsistent even inside the implementation module the tools delegate to.
fix_direction: Standardize on one verb — recommend "extract", since the internal engines already call themselves that (`comprehensive_element_cloner.extract_complete_element`, `CDPElementCloner.extract_complete_element_cdp`) — and encode the real differentiators in the name: extract_complete_element (in-response), extract_complete_element_to_file, extract_complete_element_cdp (already correctly named). Retire or clearly subordinate clone_element_complete/clone_element_to_file as deprecated aliases. Best done alongside M5's consolidation (Effort L, gated on M6) rather than as a standalone rename.

quote:
async def get_tab(
        self,
        instance_id: str,
        touch_activity: bool = True,
    ) -> Optional[Tab]:
        """
        Get the main tab for a browser instance.

        Args:
            instance_id (str): The ID of the browser instance.
            touch_activity (bool): Whether retrieving the tab should refresh last activity.

        Returns:
            Optional[Tab]: The main tab if found, else None.
        """
what: `get_tab`/`get_browser` are the two most-called accessors in the codebase — nearly every one of the 96 tools opens with `tab = await browser_manager.get_tab(instance_id)`. Both take a `touch_activity: bool = True` parameter that, on by default, updates the instance's last-activity timestamp used by the idle-timeout reaper (`touch_instance`) as a side effect of every call. The name "get_tab" promises a pure read; by default it also writes. No @section_tool-wrapped tool in server.py ever passes `touch_activity` explicitly — every call site is `browser_manager.get_tab(instance_id)` with no second argument — so this mutation is invisible not just from the tool name but from every layer an AI model would actually read; the exposed tool docstrings (e.g. execute_script's) never mention that calling the tool resets an idle timer.
why_it_matters: An agent debugging "why didn't this idle instance get reaped", or reasoning about the side effects of a call that reads like read-only (get_page_content, get_element_state, etc. all route through get_tab), has no way to discover this from the name or the exposed docstring — it would need to trace into browser_manager.py internals, which the clarity bar for this lens explicitly says shouldn't be necessary.
fix_direction: Either flip the default so the name matches behavior (`touch_activity: bool = False`, with call sites that need liveness extension opting in explicitly), or rename to signal the write (e.g. a wrapper `get_tab_and_touch` used by tool call sites, leaving a true `get_tab` read-only). At minimum, surface "refreshes the instance's idle timer" in every public tool docstring that transitively calls it.

quote:
async def get_instance_state(instance_id: str) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]:
        Returns:
            Optional[Dict[str, Any]]: Complete state information.
                return {
                    "instance_id": instance.instance_id,
                    "state": instance.state,
                    "current_url": instance.current_url,
                    "title": instance.title,
                    "source": "active",
                    "partial": True,
                    "detail_error": f"Timed out after {timeout_seconds:g}s while collecting full page state.",
                }
what: The docstring's Returns contract says "Complete state information" — but the function has two other return paths (TimeoutError and generic Exception, lines 1498-1533) explicitly designed to return a *partial* record instead: `{"instance_id", "state", "current_url", "title", "source": "active", "partial": True, "detail_error": "..."}`, or an even thinner `{"instance_id", "state": "unknown", "partial": True, "detail_error": "..."}` when the instance isn't found in the fallback list either (lines 1510-1515). Neither the `partial` flag, the `detail_error` key, nor the possibility of a degraded/missing-field response is mentioned anywhere in the docstring.
why_it_matters: An agent (or the calling model) reading only the docstring's contract — "Complete state information" — has no reason to defensively check for a `partial` flag before trusting `current_url`/`title`, and no idea `detail_error` exists as a key to surface on failure. On a tool named specifically for introspecting instance health, the one caller who most needs to know "this response is degraded" is told the opposite by the docs.
fix_direction: Document the actual return contract — note the partial/detail_error/source keys and the conditions that produce them — or split into an explicitly-typed union (e.g. a `partial: bool` field documented as the caller's cue to treat the rest as best-effort).
